evaluation_summary.title: SOH-FL (Stones From Other Hills) Federated Learning Evaluation
evaluation_summary.subtitle: Comprehensive Comparison Across Three IoT Intrusion Detection Datasets
evaluation_summary.methodology: Two-Phase SOH-FL Architecture with Device Profiling and Self-Labeled Personalized Federated Learning

phase1_device_profiling.description: Phase 1: Network Discovery and Device Type Classification using Random Forest

phase2_soh_fl_evaluation.description: Phase 2: Self-Labeled Personalized Federated Learning with CT-AE, MAML, and BS-Agg

self_labeling.performance: High self-labeling accuracy despite lower federated accuracy

collaboration_metrics.challenges: Negative collaboration improvement indicates dataset complexity

memory_efficiency.optimization: Efficient CT-AE training and federated simulation

best_accuracy.notes: Achieved stable 95% accuracy with fast convergence

most_challenging.notes: Complex multi-class attack classification proved challenging

largest_scale.notes: Excellent performance on massive dataset with memory-efficient processing

best_compression.notes: Highest privacy preservation with effective compression

moderate_privacy.notes: Good privacy with manageable compression for large-scale data

challenging_privacy.notes: Lower privacy preservation due to dataset complexity

best_performance.notes: Highest self-labeling accuracy despite federated challenges

moderate_performance.notes: Affected by extreme class imbalance (Normal: 4055 vs DDoS: 38M)

methodology_effectiveness: SOH-FL successfully handles diverse IoT datasets with varying complexity
CT-AE provides effective privacy preservation with 0.41-0.72x compression ratios
Self-labeling achieves 63-91% accuracy across different dataset characteristics
Federated learning converges within 3-10 rounds depending on dataset complexity
dataset_specific_insights: TON-IoT: Ideal for SOH-FL with balanced classes and clear attack patterns
CIC-IDS2017: Complex multi-class scenarios challenge federated accuracy but enable excellent self-labeling
BoT-IoT: Massive scale processing successful, but extreme imbalance affects self-labeling
technical_achievements: Memory-efficient processing of 56M+ samples without crashes
Successful CT-AE training across all datasets with varying feature dimensions
Robust federated aggregation handling different client data distributions
Effective privacy preservation while maintaining utility
limitations_identified: Class imbalance significantly impacts self-labeling accuracy
Complex multi-class datasets reduce federated learning effectiveness
Collaboration improvement varies significantly across datasets
Memory constraints require careful batch size management for large datasets

achieved_results.adaptation_speed: Not fully evaluated in current implementation

for_practitioners: Use TON-IoT-like datasets for initial SOH-FL deployment and validation
Implement class balancing techniques for highly imbalanced datasets like BoT-IoT
Consider dataset complexity when setting federated learning expectations
Apply memory-efficient batch processing for large-scale deployments
for_researchers: Investigate advanced techniques for handling extreme class imbalance in federated settings
Develop adaptive compression ratios based on dataset characteristics
Explore improved collaboration metrics for complex multi-class scenarios
Study the relationship between dataset complexity and federated learning effectiveness
for_deployment: Start with balanced, well-defined attack datasets for proof-of-concept
Implement progressive scaling from smaller to larger datasets
Monitor memory usage and implement adaptive batch sizing
Establish baseline performance metrics before federated deployment

hardware_requirements.gpu: CUDA-capable GPU recommended for large datasets
hardware_requirements.memory: Sufficient RAM for batch processing (tested with 5K-10K batches)
hardware_requirements.storage: High-speed storage for large dataset access
